q: Two cars A and B begin from rest and travel along a straight road for 10 s. Car A has acceleration 10 ms-2 during the first 5 s, and then 5 ms-2 during the next 5 s. Car B has acceleration 5 ms-2 during the first 5 s, and then 10 ms-2 during the next 5 s. If the distance covered by car A is $s_A$ and by car B is $s_B$, then:
question: Two cars A and B begin from rest and travel along a straight road for 10 s. Car A has acceleration 10 ms-2 during the first 5 s, and then 5 ms-2 during the next 5 s. Car B has acceleration 5 ms-2 during the first 5 s, and then 10 ms-2 during the next 5 s. If the distance covered by car A is $s_A$ and by car B is $s_B$, then:
solution:
For car A

Phase 1 ($t = 0$ to $5 ext{ s}$)

The initial velocity is $u_{A1} = 0$ and the constant acceleration is $a_{A1} = 10 ext{ ms}^{-2}$

Using equation of motion;

$$s_{A1} = u_{A1}t_1 + \frac{1}{2}a_{A1}t_1^2 = 0 + \frac{1}{2}(10)(5)^2 = 125\text{ m}$$

Using first equation of motion, the velocity at $t = 5 ext{ s}$ is

$$v_{A5} = u_{A1} + a_{A1}t_1 = 0 + 10(5) = 50\text{ ms}^{-1}$$

This velocity will be the initial velocity of car A for the second phase of journey.

Phase 2 ($t = 5$ to $10 ext{ s}$)

The initial velocity is $u_{A2} = 50\text{ ms}^{-1}$, and the constant acceleration is $a_{A2} = 5\text{ ms}^{-2}$

$$s_{A2} = u_{A2}t_2 + \frac{1}{2}a_{A2}t_2^2 = 50(5) + \frac{1}{2}(5)(5)^2 = 250 + 62.5 = 312.5\text{ m}$$

So, the total distance covered by car A in 10 seconds is,

$$s_A = 125 + 312.5 = 437.5\text{ m}$$

For car B

Phase 1 ($t = 0$ to $5 ext{ s}$)

The initial velocity is $u_{B1} = 0$ and the constant acceleration is $a_{B1} = 5\text{ ms}^{-2}$

Using equation of motion;

$$s_{B1} = u_{B1}t_1 + \frac{1}{2}a_{B1}t_1^2 = 0 + \frac{1}{2}(5)(5)^2 = 62.5\text{ m}$$

Using first equation of motion, the velocity at $t = 5\text{ s}$ is

$$v_{B5} = u_{B1} + a_{B1}t_1 = 0 + 5(5) = 25\text{ ms}^{-1}$$

This velocity will be the initial velocity of car B for the second phase of journey.

Phase 2 ($t = 5$ to $10 ext{ s}$)

The initial velocity is $u_{B2} = 25 ext{ ms}^{-1}$, and the constant acceleration is $a_{B2} = 10 ext{ ms}^{-2}$

$$s_{B2} = u_{B2}t_2 + \frac{1}{2}a_{B2}t_2^2 = 25(5) + \frac{1}{2}(10)(5)^2 = 125 + 125 = 250\text{ m}$$

So, the total distance covered by car B in 10 seconds is,

$$s_B = 62.5 + 250 = 312.5\text{ m}$$

Therefore, the distance travelled by car A is greater than the distance covered by car B, $s_A > s_B$. Hence, the correct option is (A).

q: A particle moves along $x$-axis in positive direction. Its acceleration ${ }^{\prime} a^{\prime}$ is given as $a=c x+d$, where $x$ denotes the $\mathbf{x}$-coordinate of particle, $\mathbf{c}$ and $\mathbf{d}$ are positive constants. For velocity-position graph of particle to be of type as shown in figure, find the value of speed (in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ ) of particle at $x=0$. Take $c=1 \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ and $\mathrm{d}=3 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
question: A particle moves along $x$-axis in positive direction. Its acceleration ${ }^{\prime} a^{\prime}$ is given as $a=c x+d$, where $x$ denotes the $\mathbf{x}$-coordinate of particle, $\mathbf{c}$ and $\mathbf{d}$ are positive constants. For velocity-position graph of particle to be of type as shown in figure, find the value of speed (in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ ) of particle at $x=0$. Take $c=1 \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ and $\mathrm{d}=3 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
solution:
The acceleration depends on position according to the linear relationship:

$$a = cx + d$$

As acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time $a = \frac{dv}{dt}$.

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{dv}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}$$

Since $\frac{dx}{dt} = v$ (instantaneous velocity),

$$a = v\frac{dv}{dx}$$

$$v\frac{dv}{dx} = cx + d$$

$$v\,dv = (cx + d)\,dx$$

Let the velocity of the particle at the starting position $x = 0$ be $v_0$. At any general position $x$, let the velocity be $v$. Integrating both sides

$$\int\limits_{v_0}^{v} v\,dv = \int\limits_{0}^{x} (cx + d)\,dx$$

$$\left[\frac{v^2}{2}\right]_{v_0}^{v} = \left[\frac{cx^2}{2} + dx\right]_{0}^{x}$$

$$\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{v_0^2}{2} = \frac{cx^2}{2} + dx$$

$$v^2 - v_0^2 = cx^2 + 2dx$$

$$v^2 = cx^2 + 2dx + v_0^2 \ldots (i)$$

The graph of velocity-position is a straight line with a positive vertical intercept and a positive slope. Using a straight-line equation of the form $v = mx + k$, where $m$ is the positive slope and $k$ is the positive intercept at $x = 0$ ($k = v_0$).

$$v^2 = (mx + k)^2 = m^2x^2 + 2mkx + k^2 \, ... \, (ii)$$

For $v^2 = cx^2 + 2dx + v_0^2$ to represent a perfect straight line,

Coefficient of $x^2$, $m^2 = c \rightarrow m = \sqrt{c} \, ... \, (iii)$

Coefficient of $x$, $2mk = 2d \rightarrow mk = d \, ... \, (iv)$

Constant term, $k^2 = v_0^2 \rightarrow k = v_0 \, ... \, (v)$

Substituting the expressions for $m$ and $k$ into the equation (iv):

$$(\sqrt{c})(v_0) = d$$

$$v_0 = \frac{d}{\sqrt{c}}$$

Given values are, $c = 1\ \mathrm{s}^{-2}$ and $d = 3\ \mathrm{m/s}^2$.

$$v_0 = \frac{3}{\sqrt{1}} = \frac{3}{1} = 3\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

Therefore, the speed of the particle at $x = 0$ is $3\ \mathrm{m/s}$.

q: Two cars start off to race with velocities 4 m/s and 2 m/s and travel in straight line with uniform accelerations 1 m/s2 and 2 m/s2 respectively. If they reach the final point at the same instant, then the length of the path is
question: Two cars start off to race with velocities 4 m/s and 2 m/s and travel in straight line with uniform accelerations 1 m/s2 and 2 m/s2 respectively. If they reach the final point at the same instant, then the length of the path is
solution:
Let s be the length of the path (total distance) and t be the total time taken to reach the final point.

For Car 1:

The initial velocity is $u_1 = 4\ \mathrm{m/s}$

The acceleration is $a_1 = 1\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$

For Car 2:

The initial velocity is $u_2 = 2\ \mathrm{m/s}$

The acceleration is $a_2 = 2\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$

Using second equation of motion

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Equation for Car 1:

$$s = 4t + \frac{1}{2}(1)t^2 \Rightarrow s = 4t + 0.5t^2\ \ldots\ (i)$$

Equation for Car 2:

$$s = 2t + \frac{1}{2}(2)t^2 \rightarrow s = 2t + t^2 \ldots \text{(ii)}$$

Since the distance $(s)$ is the same for both cars,

$$4t + 0.5t^2 = 2t + t^2$$

$$\Rightarrow 4t - 2t = t^2 - 0.5t^2$$

$$\Rightarrow 2t = 0.5t^2 \rightarrow 2 = 0.5t$$

$$t = \frac{2}{0.5} = 4\ \text{s}$$

Substituting the value of $t = 4\ \text{s}$ back into equation (ii),

$$s = 2(4) + (4)^2 = 8 + 16 = 24\ \text{m}$$

Therefore, the length of the path is 24 m. Hence, the correct option is (D).

q:
The graph shows a linear relation between variable y and x. Consider two quantities p and q defined by the equations.

As x changes from zero to a, which of the following statements are correct according to the graph?
question:
The graph shows a linear relation between variable y and x. Consider two quantities p and q defined by the equations.

As x changes from zero to a, which of the following statements are correct according to the graph?
options: Quantity p decreases and q remain constant.
Quantity p increases and q remain constant.
solution:
The graph shows a straight line relating the variables x and y. A general linear relationship is given by the slope-

intercept equation of a straight line:

$$y = mx + c$$

When $x = 0$, the value of $y$ is $b$. This means the $y$-intercept $c = b$.

The line slopes upward as $x$ increases from $0$ to $a$, which means the slope $m$ is positive ($m > 0$).

Therefore, the equation of the line can be written as:

$$y = mx + b ext{ (where } m > 0 ext{)}$$

The quantity $q$ is defined as:

$$q = \frac{y-b}{x}$$

Substituting the line equation $y = mx + b$ into the expression for $q$:

$$q = \frac{(mx+b)-b}{x}$$

$$q = \frac{mx}{x}$$

$$q = m$$

Since the slope $m$ of a straight line is constant everywhere, the value of $q$ remains constant as $x$ changes from $0$ to $a$.

The quantity p is defined as:

$$p = \frac{y}{x}$$

Substituting the line equation $y = mx + b$ into the expression for $p$:

$$p = \frac{mx + b}{x}$$

$$p = m + \frac{b}{x}$$

As x increases, the denominator in the term $\frac{b}{x}$ becomes larger. Since $b$ is a positive constant as seen from its position above the origin on the $y$-axis, the fraction $\frac{b}{x}$ decreases as x increases.

Because $m$ is a constant and the term $\frac{b}{x}$ is decreasing, the total value of $p = m + \frac{b}{x}$ must decrease.

Therefore, quantity $p$ decreases and quantity $q$ remains constant. Thus, the correct option is (C).

q: Your friend driving his car overtakes your car on the highway. Which of the following statement must be true at the instant he is passing you? Assume the cars as point particles.
question: Your friend driving his car overtakes your car on the highway. Which of the following statement must be true at the instant he is passing you? Assume the cars as point particles.
options: Your speed and his speed are the same.
Your acceleration and his acceleration are the same.
Your position on the highway is the same as his position on the highway.
He also observes that you overtake his car.
solution:
For an object to overtake another, it must be moving faster than the object being overtaken immediately before and during the pass.

If both cars had the exact same speed $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\text {friend }}=\mathrm{v}_{\text {you }}\right)$, the distance between them would remain constant, and no overtaking could occur. Therefore, the statement in option (A) is false.

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Overtaking can happen at constant speeds ($a = 0$ for both), while one is braking, or while one is speeding up. Therefore, the statement in option (B) is false.

Overtaking means that one object transitions from being behind another object to being ahead of it. For a continuous trajectory in space, a point particle cannot move from behind an object to ahead of it without occupying the exact same coordinate at the instant of passing.

Let $x_{\mathrm{you}}(t)$ and $x_{\mathrm{friend}}(t)$ represent the positions along the highway as a function of time $t$.

Before passing ($t < t_{\mathrm{pass}}$), $x_{\mathrm{friend}} < x_{\mathrm{you}}$

After passing ($t > t_{\mathrm{pass}}$), $x_{\mathrm{friend}} > x_{\mathrm{you}}$

At an instant $t = t_{\mathrm{pass}}$

$$x_{\mathrm{friend}}(t_{\mathrm{pass}}) = x_{\mathrm{you}}(t_{\mathrm{pass}})$$

Therefore, their positions on the highway must be same at that instant. Thus, statement in option (c) is true.

From your friend's reference frame, his car is stationary.

$$v_{yF} = v_y - v_F$$

As $v_y < v_F$ so $v_{yF} < 0$ is negative which shows that your friends observers car approaching him from the front (in his rear mirror) and moving backward relative to him. He observes you falling behind, not overtaking him. Therefore, this statement in option (D) is false.

Therefore, the correct option is (C).

q: Which of the following pair of graphs represents motion of the same particle in the same time interval?
question: Which of the following pair of graphs represents motion of the same particle in the same time interval?
solution:
Velocity ($v$) is defined as the instantaneous rate of change of position ($x$) with respect to time ($t$):

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

Velocity at any instant $t$ = Slope of the tangent line to the $x - t$ graph at that instant.

If the x-t graph is a straight line with a constant upward slope, the velocity must be constant and positive ($v > 0$).

If the $x - t$ graph is a parabola opening upwards, the slope is increasing, meaning the velocity is increasing linearly (constant positive acceleration).

If the slope of the x-t graph changes sign (e.g., goes from positive to negative), the velocity must cross zero and change sign accordingly.

If the velocity time graph is horizontal and straight, meaning velocity is constant $(\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{c}$, where $\mathrm{c}>0)$.

As the velocity is constant and positive, $\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{ct}+\mathrm{x}_0$

This is the equation for a straight line with a constant positive slope ( $c$ ) and a vertical intercept ( $x_0$ ).

The curve is a parabola bending upwards, which means the slope $\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)$ is steadily increasing over time. An increasing slope means increasing velocity (accelerated motion).

Since the first graph of option (A) shows constant velocity and the second shows changing velocity, they do not match. Hence, the option (A) is not a correct match.

The line is straight, starts above the origin (at an initial velocity $v_0 > 0$), and slopes uniformly upward.

At $t = 0$, the particle already has a positive velocity.

Because the line is straight and slopes upward, the slope $$\left(\frac{dv}{dt}\right)$$ is a constant positive value.

The velocity increases linearly over time $$\left(v = \frac{dx}{dt} = v_0 + at\right).$$

$$\int dx = \int \left(v_0 + at\right)dt$$

$$x(t) = x_0 + v_0 t + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

This is the equation of a parabola opening upward.

At $t = 0$, the curve must start with a non-zero positive slope because the initial velocity is not zero. As time goes on, the curve gets steeper and steeper (positive and constant acceleration).

So, the $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{t}$ graph in option (B), shows a particle that starts with an initial speed and continuously accelerates. Therefore, both graphs describe the same motion. Thus, option (B) is a correct match.

The line is straight, slopes upward, but starts ly at the origin $(0,0)$.

At $t = 0$, the particle starts from rest $(v = 0)$.

The velocity increases uniformly from zero $(v = at)$.

Integrating $$\int dx = \int at\,dt$$ gives:

$$x(t) = x_0 + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

This is also a parabola opening upward.

Because it starts from rest $(v_0 = 0)$, the curve must start completely flat $(\text{slope} = 0)$ at $t = 0$ and then curve upward. So, the $x-t$ graph will be obtained as,

But the $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{t}$ graph in option (C) shows a straight, inclined line starting from the origin. A straight line means the slope is completely constant, which represents constant, unchanging velocity ( $\mathrm{v}=$ constant). Thus, option (C) is not a correct match.

The line starts high on the vertical axis and slopes straight down until it crosses the time axis.

The particle starts with a positive velocity. The slope is constant and negative, meaning the particle is decelerating uniformly ($v = v_0 - at$) until its velocity reaches zero at the end of the time interval.

The velocity stays positive the entire time; it just decreases.

Integrating a uniformly decreasing velocity function

$$\int\limits{} dx = \int\limits{} v\,dt = \int\limits{} (v_0 - at)dt:$$

$$x(t) = x_0 + v_0 t - \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

This is an inverted parabola which starts with a steep positive slope (high initial velocity) and gradually flattens out until the peak of the curve is reached. At the peak, the slope becomes completely horizontal (velocity = 0). The graph should stop right at that moment where particle is at peak.

The provided $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{t}$ graph in option (D) shows that the particle goes up to a peak (slowing down to rest), but then it curves back downward. A downward slope on an $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{t}$ graph means the velocity has become negative. Thus, option (D) is also not a correct match.

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

q: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its acceleration increases uniformly at the rate of 2 m/s3. Initially, both its acceleration and velocity are zero. The distance travelled by the particle during the 3rd second is :
question: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its acceleration increases uniformly at the rate of 2 m/s3. Initially, both its acceleration and velocity are zero. The distance travelled by the particle during the 3rd second is :
solution:
The rate of change of acceleration with respect to time is $j = \frac{da}{dt}$

It is given that the acceleration is increasing at a constant rate of $2\ \mathrm{m/s^3}$. Therefore $\frac{da}{dt} = 2$

To find the acceleration $a(t)$ at any time $t$, using initial condition at $t = 0$, acceleration and velocity were zero,

$a_0 = 0\ \mathrm{m/s^2}, v_0 = 0\mathrm{m/s}$

$$da = 2\,dt \Rightarrow \int\limits_{a_0}^{a} da = \int\limits_{0}^{t} 2dt$$

$$a - 0 = 2t \Rightarrow a(t) = 2t$$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 2t \rightarrow dv = 2t\,dt$$

The initial velocity is also zero ($v_0 = 0$ at $t = 0$), so integrating to any time $t$:

$$\int\limits_{v_0}^{v} dv = \int\limits_{0}^{t} 2t\,dt$$

$$v - 0 = \left[\frac{2t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{t} \Rightarrow v(t) = t^2$$

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement with respect to time

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dt} = t^2 \Rightarrow dx = t^2\,dt$$

The distance covered during the 3rd second is the displacement that between the time $t = 2\ \mathrm{s}$ and $t = 3\ \mathrm{s}$:

$$\Delta x = \int\limits_{2}^{3} t^2\,dt$$

$$\Delta x = \left[\frac{t^3}{3}\right]_{2}^{3}$$

$$\Delta x = \frac{3^3}{3} - \frac{2^3}{3}$$

$$\Delta x = \frac{27 - 8}{3} = \frac{19}{3}\ \mathrm{m}$$

Therefore, the distance covered by the particle in the 3rd second is $\frac{19}{3}\ \mathrm{m}$. Thus, the correct option is (A).

q: A horse is galloping forward with an acceleration of $3~\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2$. Which of the following statement is necessarily true?
question: A horse is galloping forward with an acceleration of $3~\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2$. Which of the following statement is necessarily true?
options: The horse is increasing its speed by $3~\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$ every second i.e. from $0$ to $3~\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$ to $6~\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$ to $9~\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$.
The speed of the horse will triple every second i.e. from $0$ to $3~\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$ to $9~\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$ to $27~\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$.
Starting from rest, the horse will cover $3~\mathrm{m}$ of ground in the first second.
In the third second the horse will cover $9~\mathrm{m}$ of ground.
solution:
The horse is moving with a constant acceleration $a = 3\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$.

Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity with respect to time:

$$a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$$

An acceleration of $3\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$ means that the velocity changes by $3\ \mathrm{m/s}$ during every 1 second interval.

If the horse is accelerating forward, its speed increases by $3\ \mathrm{m/s}$ every second.

If it starts from rest ($v = 0$):

At $t = 1\ \mathrm{s}$, speed $= 0 + 3 = 3\ \mathrm{m/s}$

At $t = 2\ \mathrm{s}$, speed $= 3 + 3 = 6\ \mathrm{m/s}$

At $t = 3\ \mathrm{s}$, speed $= 6 + 3 = 9\ \mathrm{m/s}$

Therefore, the statement of option (A) is correct.

So, the speed increases $+3\ \mathrm{m/s}$ each second, not multiplicatively. Tripling would imply an exponential increase $v = 3^t$, which contradicts a constant acceleration value. Therefore, the statement in option (B) is incorrect.

The equation for the distance covered from rest ($u = 0$) using Newton's second equation of motion:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Substituting $u = 0$, $a = 3\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$, and $t = 1\ \mathrm{s}$:

$$s_1 = 0(1) + \frac{1}{2}(3)(1)^2 = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5\ \mathrm{m}$$

The horse covers $1.5\ \mathrm{m}$ of ground, not $3\ \mathrm{m}$. Therefore, the statement in option (C) is incorrect.

The distance covered specifically during the $n^{\mathrm{th}}$ second is given by the formula:

$$s_n = u + \frac{a}{2}(2n - 1)$$

Distance in $3^{\mathrm{rd}}$ second = (Distance in 3 seconds) - (Distance in 2 seconds)

$$s(3) = \frac{1}{2}(3)(3)^2 = \frac{27}{2} = 13.5\ \mathrm{m}$$

$$s(2)=\frac{1}{2}(3)(2)^2=\frac{12}{2}=6.0\ \text{m}$$

$$\Delta\ s=13.5\ \text{m}-6.0\ \text{m}=7.5\ \text{m}$$

The distance covered is $7.5\ \text{m}$, not $9\ \text{m}$. Therefore, the statement in option (D) is incorrect.

Therefore, the only statement which is correct is option (A).

q: The displacement x of a particle moving along a straight line, at time t is given by $x = \frac{a}{b} (1 - e^{-bt})$ where a and b are real positive constants. Therefore, which option is incorrect?
question: The displacement x of a particle moving along a straight line, at time t is given by $x = \frac{a}{b} (1 - e^{-bt})$ where a and b are real positive constants. Therefore, which option is incorrect?
options: the graph of velocity vs displacement is a straight line with negative slope
the velocity and acceleration of the particle at $t = 0$ are $a$ and $(-ab)$ respectively
the particle cannot reach a point at a distance $x'$ from its starting point if $x' > \frac{a}{b}$
the particle will return to its straight point $t \to \infty$
solution:
The given displacement function is

$$x = \frac{a}{b}(1 - e^{-bt}) = \frac{a}{b} - \frac{a}{b}e^{-bt}$$

Velocity is the first derivative of displacement with respect to time:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(ab - abe^{-bt}\right)$$

Since $ab$ is a constant, its derivative is

$$\frac{d(db)}{dt} = 0.$$

$$v = 0 - \frac{a}{b}\left(-b e^{-bt}\right) = a e^{-bt}$$

From the displacement equation,

$$x = \frac{a}{b} - \frac{a}{b}e^{-bt} \rightarrow \frac{a}{b}e^{-bt} = \frac{a}{b} - x$$

$$ae^{-bt} = a - bx$$

Since $v = ae^{-bt}$,

$$v = -bx + a$$

This is an equation of a straight line $(y = mx + c)$ where the independent variable is displacement $(x)$, the slope is $m = -b$ (a negative constant), and the vertical intercept is $c = a$. Thus, option (a) is correct..

Acceleration is the derivative of velocity with respect to time:

$$a_{acc} = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(ae^{-bt}\right) = a\left(-b e^{-bt}\right) = -ab e^{-bt}$$

Initial velocity $v(0) = ae^{-b(0)} = a(1) = a$

Initial acceleration $a_{acc}(0) = -ab e^{-b(0)} = -ab(1) = -ab$

So, the initial velocity at $t = 0$ is $a$ and acceleration $-ab$. Thus, option (b) is also correct

As $t \rightarrow \infty$; $x_{\infty} = \lim\limits_{t \rightarrow \infty} \frac{a}{b} (1 - e^{-bt})$

Since $b > 0$, as $t \rightarrow \infty$, the term $e^{-bt} \rightarrow 0$:

$$x_{\infty} = \frac{a}{b}(1 - 0) = \frac{a}{b}$$

So, the maximum position the particle approaches is $\frac{a}{b}$ but never crosses it. Therefore, it cannot reach any point $x'$ if $x' > \frac{a}{b}$. Thus, option (c) is also correct.

Since $x \rightarrow \frac{a}{b}$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$, it stays far away from its initial position $(x = 0)$. Thus, option (d) is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct options are (a), (b), and (c).

q: If $\vec{r}$ is the position vector of a particle and $\vec{v}$ its velocity then :
question: If $\vec{r}$ is the position vector of a particle and $\vec{v}$ its velocity then :
solution:
Let the position of a particle be r unit from the origin and its unit direction vector $\hat{\mathrm{r}}$ :

$$ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=r \hat{\mathrm{r}} $$

Velocity $\vec{v}$ is the time rate of change of the position vector:

$$\vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$$

Substituting $\vec{r} = r\hat{r}$,

$$\vec{v} = \frac{d}{dt}(r\hat{r})$$

Using product rule for differentiation,

$$\vec{v} = \frac{dr}{dt}\hat{r} + r\frac{d\hat{r}}{dt}$$

The vector dot product of the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ with the unit position vector $\hat{r}$:

$$\vec{v} \cdot \hat{r} = \left(\frac{dr}{dt}\hat{r} + r\frac{d\hat{r}}{dt}\right) \cdot \hat{r}$$

$$\vec{v} \cdot \hat{r} = \frac{dr}{dt}(\hat{r} \cdot \hat{r}) + r\left(\frac{d\hat{r}}{dt} \cdot \hat{r}\right)$$

The dot product of any unit vector with itself is 1:

$$\hat{r} \cdot \hat{r} = |\hat{r}|^2 = 1^2 = 1$$

Since $\hat{r}$ is a unit vector, its magnitude is constant $|\hat{r}| = 1$, so $|\hat{r}|^2 = \hat{r} \cdot \hat{r} = 1$.

$$\frac{d}{dt}(\hat{r} \cdot \hat{r}) = \frac{d}{dt}(1) = 0$$

$$\frac{d\hat{r}}{dt} \cdot \hat{r} + \hat{r} \cdot \frac{d\hat{r}}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow 2\left(\frac{d\hat{r}}{dt} \cdot \hat{r}\right) = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{d\hat{r}}{dt} \cdot \hat{r} = 0$$

Substituting into equation for $\vec{v} \cdot \hat{r}$:

$$\vec{v} \cdot \hat{r} = \frac{dr}{dt}(1) + r(0)$$

$$\vec{v} \cdot \hat{r} = \frac{dr}{dt}$$

Therefore, the correct option is (B).

q: Rectilinear motion of a particle is defined by the equation $v^3 = x^2$ (where v is velocity). The acceleration of the particle when $x = 8m$ is :
question: Rectilinear motion of a particle is defined by the equation $v^3 = x^2$ (where v is velocity). The acceleration of the particle when $x = 8m$ is :
solution:
The given relation between velocity $(v)$ and displacement $(x)$ is $$v^3=x^2$$

$$\Rightarrow v=(x^2)^{1/3}=x^{2/3}$$

Acceleration $(a)$ is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time $(t)$:

$$a=\frac{dv}{dt}$$

Since velocity is given as a function of position $x$

$$a=\frac{dv}{dx}\cdot\frac{dx}{dt}=v\frac{dv}{dx}$$

$$a=v\frac{dv}{dx}=(x^{2/3})\left[\frac{2}{3}x^{\frac{2}{3}-1}\right]$$

$$a=\frac{2}{3}x^{\frac{2}{3}}\frac{1}{3}$$

$$a=\frac{2}{3}x^{1/3}$$

At the position $x=8\ \mathrm{m}$.

$$a=\frac{2}{3}(8)^{1/3}$$

$$a=\frac{2}{3}\cdot2=\frac{4}{3}\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

Therefore, the acceleration of the particle when $x=8\ \mathrm{m}$ is $\frac{4}{3}\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$. Thus, option (C) is correct.

q: A particle is moving in a straight line with initial velocity u and uniform acceleration f. If the sum of the distances travelled in tth and (t + 1)th seconds is 100 cm, then its velocity after t seconds, in cm/s, is :
question: A particle is moving in a straight line with initial velocity u and uniform acceleration f. If the sum of the distances travelled in tth and (t + 1)th seconds is 100 cm, then its velocity after t seconds, in cm/s, is :
solution:
The distance covered in the $n^{\text{th}}$ second ($S_n$) is equal to the total displacement between $t=n$ seconds and the $t=(n-1)$ seconds:

$$S_n = X(n) - X(n-1)$$

Using the second equation of motion for uniform acceleration ($X(t)=ut+\frac{1}{2}at^2$), where the acceleration is as $f$:

$$S_n = \left[un + \frac{1}{2}fn^2\right] - \left[u(n-1) + \frac{1}{2}f(n-1)^2\right]$$

$$S_n = un + \frac{1}{2}fn^2 - \left[un - u + \frac{1}{2}f(n^2 - 2n + 1)\right]$$

$$S_n = un + \frac{1}{2}fn^2 - un + u - \frac{1}{2}fn^2 + fn - \frac{1}{2}f$$

$$S_n = u + \frac{f}{2}(2n - 1)$$

For the $t^{\text{th}}$ second ($n=t$):

$$S_t = u + \frac{f}{2}(2t - 1)$$

For the $(t+1)^{\text{th}}$ second ($n=t+1$):

$$S_{t+1} = u + \frac{f}{2}(2(t+1) - 1) = u + \frac{f}{2}(2t + 1)$$

The sum of these two distances is equal to $100\text{cm}$:

$$S_t + S_{t+1} = 100$$

$$\left[u + \frac{f}{2}(2t - 1)\right] + \left[u + \frac{f}{2}(2t)\right] = 100$$

$$(u+u) + \frac{f}{2}(2t - 1 + 2t + 1) = 100$$

$$2u + f(2t) = 100$$

$$2(u+ft) = 100$$

$$u + ft = 50$$

The final velocity ($v$) of a particle after a time interval $t$ is given by first equation of motion $v = u + at$

The acceleration is $a = f$, the velocity after $t$ seconds is:

$$v = u + ft = 50\ \text{cm/s}$$

Therefore, the velocity of the particle after $t$ seconds is $50\text{cm/s}$. Thus, the correct option is (C).

q: The motion of a body falling from rest in a resisting medium is described by the equation $\frac{dv}{dt} = a - bv$ where $a$ and $b$ are constants. The velocity at any time $t$ is :
question: The motion of a body falling from rest in a resisting medium is described by the equation $\frac{dv}{dt} = a - bv$ where $a$ and $b$ are constants. The velocity at any time $t$ is :
solution:
The motion of body is given by differential equation,

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = a - bv$$

To find the velocity $v$ as a function of time $t$,

$$\frac{dv}{a-bv} = dt$$

At time $t=0$, the initial velocity is $v=0$ because body started falling from rest.

At any time $t$, the velocity is $v$.

$$\int\limits_{0}^{v} \frac{dv}{a-bv} = \int\limits_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\left[\frac{\ln|a-bv|}{-b}\right]_{0}^{v} = [t]_{0}^{t}$$

$$[\ln|a-bv|]_{0}^{v} = -bt$$

$$\ln|a-bv| - \ln|a-b(0)| = -bt$$

$$\ln(a-bv) - \ln(a) = -bt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{a-bv}{a}\right) = -bt$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{a-bv}{a} = e^{-bt}$$

$$\Rightarrow a-bv = ae^{-bt}$$

$$\Rightarrow v = \frac{a}{b}\left(1-e^{-bt}\right)$$

Therefore, the velocity function at any time $t$ is $v_t = \frac{a}{b}(1-e^{-bt})$. Thus, the correct option is (A).

q:
The displacement $x$ of a particle depend on time $t$ as $x=\alpha t^2-\beta t^3$

(A) particle will return to its starting point after time $\frac{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$

(B) the particle will come to rest after time $\frac{\mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{\alpha}}{\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{\beta}}$

(C) the initial velocity of the particle was zero but its initial acceleration was not zero.

(D) no net force act on the particle at time $\frac{\alpha}{3 \beta}$
question:
The displacement $x$ of a particle depend on time $t$ as $x=\alpha t^2-\beta t^3$

(A) particle will return to its starting point after time $\frac{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$

(B) the particle will come to rest after time $\frac{\mathbf{2} \boldsymbol{\alpha}}{\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{\beta}}$

(C) the initial velocity of the particle was zero but its initial acceleration was not zero.

(D) no net force act on the particle at time $\frac{\alpha}{3 \beta}$
solution:
The given displacement function is $x(t)=\alpha t^2-\beta t^3$

The starting point corresponds to the position of the particle at time $t=0$:

$$x(0)=\alpha(0)^2-\beta(0)^3=0$$

Let at time $t$ the particle returns to this starting point $(x=0)$,

$$\alpha t^2-\beta t^3=0$$

$$t^2(\alpha-\beta t)=0$$

$$t^2=0 \rightarrow t=0 \quad \ldots (i) \text{ (the initial starting time)}$$

$$\alpha-\beta t=0 \rightarrow \beta t=\alpha \rightarrow t=\frac{\alpha}{\beta} \quad \ldots (ii)$$

Hence, the particle returns to its starting point after a time interval of $t=\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$. Hence, option (a) is correct.

A particle comes to rest when its instantaneous velocity becomes zero $(v=0)$.

$$v=\frac{dx}{dt}=\frac{d}{dt}(\alpha t^2-\beta t^3)=2\alpha t-3\beta t^2$$

$$2\alpha t-3\beta t^2=0$$

$$t(2\alpha-3\beta t)=0$$

$$t=0 \quad \ldots (iii) \text{ (initially at rest)}$$

$$2\alpha-3\beta t=0 \rightarrow 3\beta t=2\alpha \rightarrow t=\frac{2\alpha}{3\beta} \quad \ldots (iv)$$

Thus, the particle comes to rest again at time $t=\frac{2\alpha}{3\beta}$. So, the option (b) is also correct.

Initial velocity at $t=0$ is,

$$v(0)=2\alpha(0)-3\beta(0)^2=0$$

The acceleration is the rate of change of velocity

$$a_{\mathrm{acc}}=\frac{dv}{dt}=\frac{d}{dt}(2\alpha t-3\beta t^2)=2\alpha-6\beta t$$

So, the initial acceleration at $\mathrm{t}=0$ is

$$ \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{acc}}(0)=2 \alpha-6 \beta(0)=2 \alpha $$

Since the initial velocity is zero and the initial acceleration is $2 \alpha \neq 0$, option (c) is also correct.

According to Newton's second law $F_{\text{net}} = m \cdot a_{\text{acc}}$, the net force acting on a particle is zero when its acceleration is zero ($a_{\text{acc}} = 0$).

$$2\alpha - 6\beta t = 0$$

$$6\beta t = 2\alpha$$

$$t = \frac{2\alpha}{6\beta} = \frac{\alpha}{3\beta}$$

So, no net force acts on the particle at time $t = \frac{\alpha}{3\beta}$. So, the option (d) is also correct.

Therefore, the options (a), (b), (c), and (d) are all correct.

q: The displacement-time graph of a moving particle with constant acceleration is shown in the figure. The velocity-time graph is best given by :
question: The displacement-time graph of a moving particle with constant acceleration is shown in the figure. The velocity-time graph is best given by :
solution:
The graph given is a downward parabola opening upwards, with its lowest point (vertex) touching the time axis at $t = 1 ext{ s}$.

The position of a particle under constant acceleration $a$ can be represented by the general quadratic equation:

$$x(t) = At^2 + Bt + C$$

As the velocity $(v)$ is the rate of change of position with respect to time:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = 2At + B$$

The slope of the tangent to a displacement-time graph represents the instantaneous velocity.

At $t = 0{ s}$ since the tangent line at a peak of the graph is horizontal, the velocity at this point must be zero:

$$v(0) = 0$$

So, initially the particle is dropped or released from rest.

$$v(0) = 0 \Rightarrow 2A \times 0 + B = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0$$

So, the velocity as function of time becomes $v(t) = 2At$

For the interval $0 \leq t < 1 { s}$ the position $x$ is decreasing as time progresses. A downward-sloping curve means the slope (velocity) is negative $(v < 0)$. Which means as the particle is released it starts moving vertically downward if we assume the direction to the down as negative and to the up positive.

So, in this interval the velocity should be negative, i.e., if $A$ is a positive constant then $v(t) = -2At$.

This is the velocity equation under a constant acceleration $a = -2A$:

At $t = 1 { s}$ the graph reaches its minimum turning point.

For the interval $1 < t \leq 2\text{ s}$ the position $x$ is increasing as time progresses. An upward-sloping curve means the slope (velocity) is positive ($v > 0$). Now, the particle is moving vertically upward.

As the displacement-time graph is symmetric about vertical line $x = 1$, the velocity-time graph will also be symmetric.

$$v(t) = v_0 - 2At$$

Where $v_0$ is the velocity at $t = 1\text{ s}$

At $t = 2\text{ s}$, the slope of the graph again becomes zero, i.e., the velocity at $t = 2\text{ s}$ is zero.

The velocity can be written as piecewise function;

$$v = \begin{cases} -at & 0 < t < 1 \\ a-at & 1 < t < 2 \end{cases}$$

So, the graph of velocity-time is,

Therefore, the correct option is (A).

q:
Velocity-time $(v-t)$ graph of a particle moving in straight line is shown in the given figure.

Which of the following graphs shows variation in acceleration of the particle with time?
question:
Velocity-time $(v-t)$ graph of a particle moving in straight line is shown in the given figure.

Which of the following graphs shows variation in acceleration of the particle with time?
solution:
The given equation for velocity as a function of time:

$$v(t)=10e^{-t/2}$$

The instantaneous acceleration ($a_{\mathrm{acc}}$) is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time:

$$a_{\mathrm{acc}}=\frac{dv}{dt}$$

$$a_{\mathrm{acc}}=\frac{d}{dt}\left(10e^{-t/2}\right)=10\frac{d}{dt}\left(e^{-t/2}\right)=10\left[e^{-t/2}\cdot\frac{d}{dt}\left(-\frac{t}{2}\right)\right]=10\left[e^{-t/2}\cdot\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\right]$$

$$a_{\mathrm{acc}}=-5e^{-t/2}\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

Initial acceleration at $t=0$ is,

$$a_{\mathrm{acc}}(0)=-5e^{-0/2}=-5e^0=-5\cdot1=-5\ \mathrm{m/s^2}$$

So, the acceleration-time graph must start at a value of $-5\mathrm{m/s^2}$ on the vertical acceleration axis when $t=0\mathrm{s}$.

As time becomes infinitely large ($t\rightarrow\infty$), the exponent term $-\frac{t}{2}\rightarrow-\infty\rightarrow e^{-\infty}=0$

$$\lim\limits_{t\to\infty} a_{\mathrm{acc}}(t)=-5\times0=0$$

So, the acceleration graph must rise from its negative initial value and approach the horizontal time axis exponentially ($a_{\mathrm{acc}}=0$).

Therefore, the acceleration-time is represented correctly in option (A).

q: If a particle moves in a straight line according to the law $x = t^3 - 6t^2 - 15t$, the time interval during which velocity is negative and acceleration is positive, is
question: If a particle moves in a straight line according to the law $x = t^3 - 6t^2 - 15t$, the time interval during which velocity is negative and acceleration is positive, is
solution:
The position function with respect to time $t$ is given as $x = t^3 - 6t^2 - 15t$

Velocity is the rate of change of position with respect to time

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} \Rightarrow v = \frac{d}{dt}(t^3 - 6t^2 - 15t)$$

$$v = 3t^2 - 12t - 15$$

The velocity to be negative ($v < 0$):

$$3t^2 - 12t - 15 < 0$$

$$t^2 - 4t - 5 < 0$$

$$t^2 - 5t + t - 5 < 0$$

$$t(t - 5) + 1(t - 5) < 0$$

$$(t - 5)(t + 1) < 0$$

For the product of two linear factors to be negative, the time variable $t$ must lie between the two roots ($t = -1$ and $t = 5$):

$$-1 < t < 5$$

Since time $t$ cannot be negative for the motion starting from the origin ($t \geq 0$)

So, the required time interval where velocity is negative is,

$$0 \leq t < 5$$

Taking negative time in consideration, the interval for negative velocity is:

$$-1 < t < 5 \ldots (ii)$$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

$$a = \frac{d}{dt}(3t^2 - 12t - 15)$$

$$a = 6t - 12$$

For the acceleration to be positive ($a > 0$):

$$6t - 12 > 0$$

$$6t > 12$$

$$t > \frac{12}{6}$$

So, the required time interval where the acceleration is positive is $t > 2$ … (ii)

The time interval where both conditions are satisfied simultaneously (velocity is negative & acceleration is positive), is the intersection of both the intervals :

Combining two boundaries together $2<\mathrm{t}<5$

Therefore, the required time interval is $2<\mathrm{t}<5$. Thus, the correct option (B).

q: The velocity of a particle varies as $\vec{v} = [(2\hat{i} + (3 - t)\hat{j})] \textrm{ms}^{-1}$ where t is in seconds. Find the average velocity of the particle (i.e.,$\langle \vec{v} \rangle = \frac{\int \vec{v} dt}{\int dt}$ ) for a time interval between t = 0 and a time when the speed (i.e. $v$) of the particle becomes minimum
question: The velocity of a particle varies as $\vec{v} = [(2\hat{i} + (3 - t)\hat{j})] \textrm{ms}^{-1}$ where t is in seconds. Find the average velocity of the particle (i.e.,$\langle \vec{v} \rangle = \frac{\int \vec{v} dt}{\int dt}$ ) for a time interval between t = 0 and a time when the speed (i.e. $v$) of the particle becomes minimum
solution:
The velocity vector of the particle is given as a function of time $\vec{v}(t) = 2\hat{i} + (3 - t)\hat{j}$

The speed $v$ of the particle is the magnitude of its velocity vector:

$$v = |\vec{v}| = \sqrt{(v_x)^2 + (v_y)^2}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2^2 + (3 - t)^2} = \sqrt{4 + (3 - t)^2}$$

When the speed is to be minimized, we can minimize the expression inside the square root, $f(t) = 4 + (3 - t)^2$.

At minimum the first derivative of the function with relative to time should be zero.

$$\frac{df}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} [4 + (3 - t)^2] = 0$$

$0+2(3-t) \cdot \frac{d}{d t}(3-t)=0$

$$2(3 - t)(-1) = 0$$

$$-2(3 - t) = 0 \Rightarrow 3 - t = 0 \Rightarrow t = 3\ \text{s}$$

Thus, the speed will be minimum at $t = 3\ \text{s}$.

The average velocity for the interval between $t = 0\ \text{s}$ and $t = 3\ \text{s}$.

$$\langle \vec{v} \rangle = \frac{\int\limits_{0}^{3} \vec{v}\, dt}{\int\limits_{0}^{3} dt} = \frac{\int\limits_{0}^{3} [2\hat{i} + (3 - t)\hat{j}]\, dt}{\int\limits_{0}^{3} dt} = \left[\frac{\int\limits_{0}^{3} 2\, dt}{\int\limits_{0}^{3} dt}\right]\hat{i} + \left[\frac{\int\limits_{0}^{3} (3 - t)\, dt}{\int\limits_{0}^{3} dt}\right]\hat{j}$$

$$\langle \vec{v} \rangle = \left[\frac{2[t]_{0}^{3}}{[t]_{0}^{3}}\right]\hat{i} + \left[\frac{\left[3t - \frac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{3}}{[t]_{0}^{3}}\right]\hat{j}$$

$$\langle \vec{v} \rangle = \left[\frac{2(3 - 0)}{(3 - 0)}\right]\hat{i} + \left[\frac{\left(3 \times 3 - \frac{3^2}{2}\right) - \left(3 \times 0 - \frac{0^2}{2}\right)}{(3 - 0)}\right]\hat{j}$$

$$\langle \vec{v} \rangle = [2]\hat{i} + \left[\frac{\frac{9}{2} - 0}{3}\right]\hat{j}$$

$$\langle \vec{v} \rangle = 2\hat{i} + \frac{3}{2}\hat{j}$$

Therefore, the average velocity vector is $2\hat{i} + \frac{3}{2}\hat{j}$. Thus, the correct option is (B).

q: A person walks up a stationary escalator in time $t_1$. If he remains stationary on the escalator, then it can take him up in time $t_2$. How much time would it take him to walk up the moving escalator?
question: A person walks up a stationary escalator in time $t_1$. If he remains stationary on the escalator, then it can take him up in time $t_2$. How much time would it take him to walk up the moving escalator?
solution:
Let L be the total length of the escalator from the bottom platform to the top landing floor.

When the escalator is stationary, the person walks up the entire length L in a time interval $\mathrm{t}_1$.

The walking speed of the person relative to the escalator at rest is :

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{pe}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{t}_1} $$

If the person stands stationary on the escalator, the same distance $L$ is covered in a time interval $t_2$.

Therefore, the speed of the escalator relative to the ground is:

$$v_e = \frac{L}{t_2}$$

When the person walks on running escalator $v_{pg}$ is the velocity of person with respect to ground.

$$v_{pe} = v_{pg} - v_e$$

$$v_{pg} = v_{pe} + v_e$$

$$v_{pg} = \frac{L}{t_1} + \frac{L}{t_2}$$

$$v_{pg} = L\left(\frac{1}{t_1} + \frac{1}{t_2}\right)$$

$$v_{pg} = L\left(\frac{t_2 + t_1}{t_1 t_2}\right)$$

The time taken is when person is walking on running escalator is,

$$t_3 = \frac{L}{v_{pg}}$$

$$t_3 = \frac{L}{L/\left(\frac{t_1+t_2}{t_1 t_2}\right)}$$

$$t_3 = \frac{t_1 t_2}{t_1 + t_2}$$

Therefore, the time it takes to walk up the moving escalator is $\frac{t_1 t_2}{t_1+t_2}$. Thus, the correct option is (C).

q: The acceleration time graph of a particle moving in a straight line is as shown in figure. The velocity of the particle at time $t = 0$ is $2 \, \mathrm{m/s}$. The velocity after $2 \, \mathrm{s}$ will be
question: The acceleration time graph of a particle moving in a straight line is as shown in figure. The velocity of the particle at time $t = 0$ is $2 \, \mathrm{m/s}$. The velocity after $2 \, \mathrm{s}$ will be
solution:
The instantaneous acceleration $a$ is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time.

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

$$dv = a \cdot dt$$

Integrating both sides from an initial time $t_1$ to a final time $t_2$:

$$\int\limits_{v_1}^{v_2} dv = \int\limits_{t_1}^{t_2} a\,dt$$

$$v_2 - v_1 = \int\limits_{t_1}^{t_2} a\,dt$$

The definite integral $\int\limits_{t_1}^{t_2} a\,dt$ is equal to the area under the curve of the acceleration-time graph

$$\Delta v = v_{\text{final}} - v_{\text{initial}} = \text{Area under } a - t \text{ graph}$$

The shape under the acceleration line between $t = 0\ \text{s}$ and $t = 2\ \text{s}$ forms a triangle.

The base of the triangle is along the time axis from $0$ to $2\ \text{s}$:

$$\text{Base} = 2 - 0 = 2\ \text{s}$$

The peak height of the triangle reaches up to the maximum acceleration value on the vertical axis:

$$\text{Height} = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$$

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$$

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4 = 4\ \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the change in velocity ($\Delta v$) over this two-second interval is $4\ \text{m/s}$.

The initial velocity at $t = 0$ is $2\ \text{m/s}$.

$$\Delta v = v(2) - v(0) = v(2) - 2 = 4$$

$$v(2)=4+2=6\ \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the velocity of the particle after 2 seconds is 6 m/s. Thus, the correct option is (A).

q: A particle moves along a straight line such that its displacement is given by $x=16 t-2 t^2$. Let the distance travelled by the particle in the first 2 s be $S_1$ and in the first 6 s be $S_2$. Determine $\frac{3 S_2}{S_1}$
question: A particle moves along a straight line such that its displacement is given by $x=16 t-2 t^2$. Let the distance travelled by the particle in the first 2 s be $S_1$ and in the first 6 s be $S_2$. Determine $\frac{3 S_2}{S_1}$
solution:
The position equation is given as a function of time $t$:

$$x(t) = 16t - 2t^2$$

The instantaneous velocity $v(t)$ is the rate of change of position with respect to time:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}(16t - 2t^2) = 16 - 4t$$

A particle takes U-turn and changes its direction when its velocity becomes zero.

Let the turning point time is $t_0$:

$$16 - 4t_0 = 0$$

$$4t_0 = 16 \rightarrow t_0 = 4\ \mathrm{s}$$

For $t < 4\ \mathrm{s}$, the velocity $v(t) > 0$, i.e., the particle moves in the positive direction.

For $t > 4\ \mathrm{s}$, the velocity $v(t) < 0$, i.e., the particle turns around and moves in the negative direction.

Since the turning point occurs at $t = 4\ \mathrm{s}$, the particle moves continuously in a single direction without during the interval from $t = 0\ \mathrm{s}$ to $t = 2\ \mathrm{s}$.

When motion is uniform, the magnitude of displacement equals the distance travelled:

$$ S_1=|x(2)-x(0)| $$

Position at $\mathrm{t}=0$ is $\mathrm{x}(0)=16(0)-2(0)^2=0 \mathrm{~m}$

Position at $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is $\mathrm{x}(2)=16(2)-2(2)^2=32-8=24 \mathrm{~m}$

Thus, the distance $\mathrm{S}_1$ is:

$$ S_1=|24-0|=24 \mathrm{~m} $$

The time interval from $\mathrm{t}=0 \mathrm{~s}$ to $\mathrm{t}=6 \mathrm{~s}$ includes the turning point at $\mathrm{t}=4 \mathrm{~s}$. Therefore, the journey can be divided into two separate intervals where the motion remains in a particular direction:

From $t = 0 ext{ s}$ to $t = 4 ext{ s}$ the particle is moving forward.

From $t = 4 ext{ s}$ to $t = 6 ext{ s}$ the particle is moving backward.

The total distance $S_2$ is the sum of the absolute path lengths of these two intervals:

$$S_2 = |x(4) - x(0)| + |x(6) - x(4)|$$

The positions at $t = 4$ is $x(4) = 16(4) - 2(4)^2 = 64 - 32 = 32\text{ m}$

The positions at $t = 6$ is $x(6) = 16(6) - 2(6)^2 = 96 - 72 = 24\text{ m}$

So, the distance $S_2$ is.

$$S_2 = |32 - 0| + |24 - 32|$$

$$S_2 = 32 + |-8| = 32 + 8 = 40\text{ m}$$

The value of $\frac{3S_2}{S_1}$ is,

$$\frac{3S_2}{S_1} = 3 \times \frac{40}{24} = \frac{120}{24} = 5$$

Therefore, the value of $\frac{3S_2}{S_1}$ is 5.

q: A police jeep pursues a motor bike ridden by a culprit. The motor bike passes through a curve while maintaining a uniform speed of $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. A jeep, traveling at a steady speed of $108 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, reaches the same curve 10 seconds after the bike. Upon passing the curve, the jeep begins to accelerate at a fixed rate of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. If the motor bike continues at its initial speed, the jeep successfully intercepts it at a distance of $20 \alpha \mathrm{~m}$ from the curve. Given that $a$ represents an integer, determine the value of $\alpha$.
question: A police jeep pursues a motor bike ridden by a culprit. The motor bike passes through a curve while maintaining a uniform speed of $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. A jeep, traveling at a steady speed of $108 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, reaches the same curve 10 seconds after the bike. Upon passing the curve, the jeep begins to accelerate at a fixed rate of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. If the motor bike continues at its initial speed, the jeep successfully intercepts it at a distance of $20 \alpha \mathrm{~m}$ from the curve. Given that $a$ represents an integer, determine the value of $\alpha$.
solution:
Speed of the bike $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)$ is,

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{b}}=72 \times \frac{5}{18}=4 \times 5=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

Initial speed of the jeep $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{j}}\right)$ is,

$$ \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{j}}=108 \times \frac{5}{18}=6 \times 5=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

Let the turning point be the origin ( $\mathrm{x}=0$ ).

Let $\mathrm{t}=0$ be the instant the jeep crosses the turn.

The bike crossed the turn 10 s before the jeep. Therefore, at $t = 0$, the bike has already been moving at a constant speed of $20 \, \text{m/s}$ for $10 \, \text{s}$.

Initial position of the bike at $t = 0$:

$$x_{b0} = \text{speed} \times \text{time} = 20 \times 10 \, \text{m} = 200 \, \text{m}$$

Since the bike continues to travel at a constant speed ($20 \, \text{m/s}$), its position equation for $t \geq 0$ is:

$$x_b(t) = x_{b0} + v_b t$$

$$x_b(t) = 200 + 20t \ldots (i)$$

The jeep crosses the turn at $t = 0$, so its initial position is $x_{j0} = 0$.

After crossing the turn, it accelerates with a constant acceleration $a = 2 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

Using the second equation of motion ($s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$):

$$x_j(t) = v_j t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2$$

$$x_j(t) = 30t + \frac{1}{2}(2)t^2$$

$$x_j(t) = 30t + t^2 \ldots (ii)$$

When the jeep catches the bike, their positions are equal ($x_j(t) = x_b(t)$):

$$30t + t^2 = 200 + 20t$$

$$t^2 + 30t - 20t - 200 = 0$$

$$t^2 + 10t - 200 = 0$$

$$t^2 + 20t - 10t - 200 = 0$$

$$t(t + 20) - 10(t + 20) = 0$$

$$(t - 10)(t + 20) = 0$$

$t = 10\ \mathrm{s}$ or $t = -20\ \mathrm{s}$

As time cannot be negative, so we discard this solution.

Thus, the jeep catches the bike 10 seconds after crossing the turn.

Hence, the total distance travelled by the jeep from the turn is,

$$x_j(10) = 30(10) + (10)^2$$

$$x_j(10) = 300 + 100 = 400\ \mathrm{m} = 20\alpha\ \mathrm{m} \Rightarrow \alpha = 20$$

The jeep catches the bike after a chase time of 10 s, at a total distance of 400 m from the turn.

q: A particle moves in $x y$-plane according to the equation $x=3 t, y=25-4 t$. What is the minimum distance of the particle (in m ) from the origin? Both x and y are in m .
question: A particle moves in $x y$-plane according to the equation $x=3 t, y=25-4 t$. What is the minimum distance of the particle (in m ) from the origin? Both x and y are in m .
solution:
The coordinates of the particle at any instantaneous time $t$ are,

$$x(t)=3t$$

$$y(t)=25-4t$$

The straight-line distance ($s$) of any point $(x,y)$ from the origin $(0,0)$ is

$$s = \sqrt{(x-0)^2 + (y-0)^2} = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

Substituting the given time-dependent expressions for $x$ and $y$ into this distance formula.

$$s = \sqrt{(3t)^2 + (25-4t)^2}$$

$$s = \sqrt{9t^2 + 16t^2 - 200t + 625}$$

$$s = \sqrt{25t^2 - 200t + 625}$$

So, the distance of particle from origin is the function of time, to minimize the distance $s$, we need to minimize the function, $f(t) = 25t^2 - 200t + 625$

For maximum or minimum value of a function when its first derivative with respect to its independent variable equals zero:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}f}{\mathrm{d}t} = 0$$

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t}(25t^2 - 200t + 625) = 0$$

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}f}{\mathrm{d}t} = 50t - 200 = 0$$

$$50t - 200 = 0$$

$$50t - 200 = 0$$

$$50t = 200$$

$$t=\frac{200}{50}=4\ \mathrm{s}$$

The coordinates at $t=4\ \mathrm{s}$:

$$x=3(4)=12\ \mathrm{m}$$

$$y=25-4(4)=25-16=9\ \mathrm{m}$$

Using the distance formula at $t=4\ \mathrm{s}$ the distance of particle from the origin is minimum,

$$s_{\min}=\sqrt{12^2+9^2}$$

$$s_{\min}=\sqrt{144+81}$$

$$s_{\min}=\sqrt{225}$$

$$s_{\min}=15\ \mathrm{m}$$

Therefore, the minimum distance of the particle from the origin is $15\ \mathrm{m}$.

q: A parachutist jumps from a height of 100 m. He wants to touch the ground with zero velocity. To achieve this, he turns on a parachute propeller after falling freely through some distance. It is given that, after the propeller is turned on, the net acceleration of the man depends on his velocity as $a=-2v$, where $v$ is the instantaneous velocity of the man. Determine the time for which he should fall freely before switching on the parachute propeller for this purpose. (Take $\mathrm{g}=10\,\mathrm{m/s}^2$)
question: A parachutist jumps from a height of 100 m. He wants to touch the ground with zero velocity. To achieve this, he turns on a parachute propeller after falling freely through some distance. It is given that, after the propeller is turned on, the net acceleration of the man depends on his velocity as $a=-2v$, where $v$ is the instantaneous velocity of the man. Determine the time for which he should fall freely before switching on the parachute propeller for this purpose. (Take $\mathrm{g}=10\,\mathrm{m/s}^2$)
solution:
For the phase where parachutist is under free fall, the parachutist drops from rest ($u_0 = 0$) under the influence of gravity ($g = 10 { m/s}^2$).

Let us assume he falls freely for a time duration $t_{\text{free}}$:

$$g = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

$$\int\limits_{0}^{v_1} dv = \int\limits_{0}^{t_{\text{free}}} gdt$$

$$v_1 = g\cdot t_{\text{free}} = 10t_{\text{free}}$$

Where $v_1$ is the downward velocity achieved at the moment the propeller is switched on.

The distance fallen during free fall using $v = \frac{dy}{dt}$.

$$\int\limits_{0}^{h_1} dy = \int\limits_{0}^{t_{\text{free}}} (10t)dt$$

$$h_1 = \left[\frac{10t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{t_{\text{free}}} = 5t_{\text{free}}^2$$

Once the propeller turns on, the net acceleration changes to $a = -2v$.

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{dv}{dy}\cdot\frac{dy}{dt} = v\frac{dv}{dy}$$

$$v\frac{dv}{dy} = -2v$$

$$\frac{dv}{dy} = -2$$

$$dv = -2\,dy$$

The distance travelled during the second phase if deceleration

At the start of phase 2, the position is $y = 0$ and the velocity is $v_1$.

At the end of phase 2, the parachutist travels a distance $h_2$ and lands on the ground with a final velocity $v_2 = 0$:

$$\int\limits_{v_1}^{0} dv = \int\limits_{0}^{h_2} 2\,dy$$

$$[v]_{v_1}^{0} = -2[y]_{0}^{h_2}$$

$$0 - v_1 = -2(h_2 - 0)$$

$$-v_1 = -2h_2 \rightarrow h_2 = \frac{v_1}{2}$$

$$h_2 = \frac{10t_{\mathrm{free}}}{2} = 5t_{\mathrm{free}}$$

The total vertical height travelled across both consecutive phases must equal the initial jumping altitude of 100 m:

$$h_1 + h_2 = 100$$

$$5t_{\mathrm{free}}^2 + 5t_{\mathrm{free}} = 100$$

$$t_{\mathrm{free}}^2 + t_{\mathrm{free}} = 20$$

$$t_{\mathrm{free}}^2 + t_{\mathrm{free}} - 20 = 0$$

$$t_{\mathrm{free}}^2 + 5t_{\mathrm{free}} - 4t_{\mathrm{free}} - 20 = 0$$

$$t_{\mathrm{free}}(t_{\mathrm{free}} + 5) - 4(t_{\mathrm{free}} + 5) = 0$$

$$\left(t_{\mathrm{free}} - 4\right)\left(t_{\mathrm{free}} + 5\right) = 0$$

$t_{\mathrm{free}} = 4\ \mathrm{s}$ or $t_{\mathrm{free}} = -5\ \mathrm{s}$

Since a time duration must be a positive quantity, the time after falling freely that the man should switch on the parachute propeller is 4 seconds.

q: The maximum possible acceleration of a train starting from the rest and moving on straight track is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and maximum possible retardation is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. The maximum speed that train can achieve is $70 \frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{~s}}$. Minimum time in which the train can complete a journey of 1000 m ending at rest is $\frac{347}{2 \alpha}$ sec. Where $\alpha$ is an intger. Find $\alpha$.
question: The maximum possible acceleration of a train starting from the rest and moving on straight track is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and maximum possible retardation is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. The maximum speed that train can achieve is $70 \frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{~s}}$. Minimum time in which the train can complete a journey of 1000 m ending at rest is $\frac{347}{2 \alpha}$ sec. Where $\alpha$ is an intger. Find $\alpha$.
solution:
To minimize the total time required to travel a fixed distance $\mathrm{S}=1000 \mathrm{~m}$ starting from rest and ending at rest, the train must accelerate at its maximum possible rate $\left(\mathrm{a}_{\max }=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$ up to a peak velocity $\mathrm{v}_{\text {peak }}$, and then decelerate at its maximum possible retarding rate ($a_{ret }$$=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ) back to zero.

Let the acceleration phase cover distance $s_1$ over time $t_1$, and the deceleration phase cover distance $s_2$ over time $t_2$.

As acceleration is the rate of change of velocity:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} \Rightarrow dv = a\,dt$$

Integrating from rest $(u = 0)$ to peak velocity $v_{\text{peak}}$ under constant acceleration $a_1 = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$:

$$v_{\text{peak}} = a_1 t_1 = 10t_1 \Rightarrow t_1 = \frac{v_{\text{peak}}}{10}undefined = v_{\text{peak}} - a_2 t_2 \Rightarrow v_{\text{peak}} = 5t_2 \Rightarrow t_2 = \frac{v_{\text{peak}}}{5}$$

For constant acceleration, using equation of motion for the displacement $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$

$$s_1 = \frac{1}{2} a_1 t_1^2 = \frac{1}{2}(10)\left(\frac{v_{\text{peak}}}{10}\right)^2 = \frac{v_{\text{peak}}^2}{20}$$

$$s_2 = \frac{1}{2} a_2 t_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}(5)\left(\frac{v_{\text{peak}}}{5}\right)^2 = \frac{v_{\text{peak}}^2}{10}$$

The total displacement $S_{\text{total}}$ is 1000 m.

$$S_{\text{total}} = s_1 + s_2 = \frac{v_{\text{peak}}^2}{20} + \frac{v_{\text{peak}}^2}{10} = \frac{3v_{\text{peak}}^2}{20}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{3v_{\text{peak}}^2}{20} = 1000$$

$$\Rightarrow 3v_{\text{peak}}^2 = 20000 \Rightarrow v_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{\frac{20000}{3}} \approx \sqrt{6666.67} \approx 81.65\ \text{m/s} > 70\ \text{m/s}$$

The allowed maximum speed limit of $70\ \text{m/s}$, the train must accelerate up to $70\ \text{m/s}$, stay at this constant speed for a specific distance $s_3$ during a time interval $t_3$, and then decelerate to rest.

During acceleration from rest to maximum speed 70 m/s

$$v = a_1 t_1 \Rightarrow 70 = 10t_1 \Rightarrow t_1 = 7\ \mathrm{s}$$

$$s_1 = \frac{1}{2}a_1 t_1^2 = \frac{1}{2}(10)(7)^2 = 5 \times 49 = 245\ \mathrm{m}$$

During deceleration to rest from 70 m/s,

$$0 = v - a_2 t_2 \Rightarrow 0 = 70 - 5t_2 \Rightarrow t_2 = \frac{70}{5} = 14\ \mathrm{s}$$

$$s_2 = \frac{1}{2}a_2 t_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}(5)(14)^2 = 2.5 \times 196 = 490\ \mathrm{m}$$

So, the distance travelled during constant motion is;

$$s_3 = S_{\text{total}} - (s_1 + s_2) = 1000 - (245 + 490) = 1000 - 735 = 265\ \mathrm{m}$$

During motion with constant speed, since acceleration is zero during this phase:

$$s_3 = 70 \cdot t_3 \rightarrow 265 = 70 \cdot t_3 \Rightarrow t_3 = \frac{265}{70} = \frac{53}{14}\ \mathrm{s}$$

The total minimum time $(T_{\min})$ is the sum of the durations of all three phases:

$$T_{\min} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 7 + 14 + \frac{53}{14} = 21 + \frac{53}{14}$$

$$T_{\min} = \frac{21 \times 14 + 53}{14} = \frac{294 + 53}{14} = \frac{347}{14}\ \mathrm{s}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{347}{14} = \frac{347}{2\alpha}$$

$$\Rightarrow 2\alpha = 14 \Rightarrow \alpha = 7$$

Therefore, the value of the integer $\alpha$ is 7.
